+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 45.7
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I can’t tell if it’s the heat or just the way I’m wired, but my backpack keeps slipping off my shoulder every time I turn a corner. I finally stop walking long enough to slide it back up — again — and glance around. New Orleans. Damn. I’ve only been here for a few hours, but the air already feels thick, like I’m breathing through a sponge. My mind is a mess of tangled thoughts. Yesterday was a disaster, and today… I guess I expected something different. Maybe a little hope? But instead, I’m standing here, clutching my bag like it’s some kind of lifeline, trying not to feel like I’ve just jumped into the deep end with no floaties. I should probably find a place to crash — a shelter, a safe spot — but every step I take feels heavy, like I’m dragging a bag of rocks. Still, I keep walking, because what else is there? I’m out of options, and honestly, I don’t trust myself to sit still anymore. That’s when I see him. A guy, maybe a little older than me, sitting on a battered bench beneath a streetlamp that flickers. He’s got dark hair, a scruffy beard, and a look like he’s seen a lot but still manages to wear a crooked smile. He notices me watching and waves lazily. “Hey there,” he says, voice rough but friendly. “Lost?” I hesitate, then shrug. “Yeah. Kinda. I just… needed to get away.” He nods, like he understands more than I realize. “Nola’s got a way of swallowing people whole, huh?” “You could say that,” I reply, feeling a flicker of something I haven’t felt in a while — relief, maybe? Or just the fact that someone else is talking to me. No one in my small town talks like this, not without a hidden agenda. He shuffles closer. “Name’s Marcus. And you?” “Taylor.” I glance at him, wondering if I should trust him, but I’m running on empty. Might as well try. “Taylor. Nice name.” He grins, revealing crooked teeth. “You look like you’ve got a story.” I hesitate. Do I tell him? Do I trust him? That’s the thing about being new here. I don’t really know what the rules are. All I know is I want to breathe, to feel like I’m not just a problem waiting to happen. “I ran,” I say finally, voice tight. “From… everything. My family, the town. Just needed to get out.” Marcus’s eyes soften. “Yeah, I get that. I’ve been running, too. From my own shit.” He leans back, gaze distant. “Been on the streets a couple of weeks now. It’s a mess, but it’s better than being trapped.” I nod, feeling a little less alone. “How do you do it? Survive, I mean?” He shrugs. “You take what you get. Look out for those who look out for you. And… don’t trust too easily.” His eyes flicker with something darker, a warning I catch but don’t fully understand yet. I shift uncomfortably. “You think I can trust anyone here?” He studies me, then laughs softly. “Trust is a tricky thing, especially out here. But maybe, just maybe, you’ll find someone who won’t stab you in the back. Or worse.” I want to believe that. I really do. But my gut is telling me to stay guarded. The last two days have hammered that lesson into me. “Hey,” Marcus says, voice quieter. “You looking for a place to stay? I know a spot where it’s safe — at least for tonight. No cops, no crazy folks. Just a little hideout I found.” I consider. Part of me wants to keep moving, to keep running. But another part — a small, stubborn piece — whispers that maybe I need a break. Maybe I should stop and breathe for a second. “Yeah,” I say finally. “That sounds good.” He nods, standing up. “Follow me. It’s not much, but it’s better than sleeping on concrete.” We walk through the winding streets, the city’s chaos humming all around us. The sounds of distant jazz, the smell of fried food, the hum of life — all of it feels overwhelming but strangely comforting. Like maybe, just maybe, I’m not entirely alone in this. As we turn down a narrow alley, Marcus pauses and points to a door half-hidden behind a pile of crates. “Here. No one’s gonna bother us. Just stay low, and don’t get caught.” I nod, heart pounding. I don’t know what’s worse — the fear of getting caught or the fear of what I might find inside. Inside, it’s dark, but I can make out a small space — a mattress, some blankets, an old chair. It’s not much, but it’s enough to make me feel like I’ve found a little piece of safety. For now. Marcus kicks a box aside and plops onto the mattress. “You hungry?” I shake my head. “No. Just… tired.” He grins again, that crooked smile. “Yeah, I get it. You’ll sleep better here than out in the street. Trust me.” I stare at the ceiling, trying to block out the chaos in my mind. The last few days have been a whirlwind of emotions — shock, fear, anger, hope. I came here thinking I’d find a new life, a new beginning. But right now, all I feel is exhaustion. And yet, deep inside, there’s this tiny spark — stubborn, fierce, unbreakable. I’ve made it this far. I’ve run from everything that’s tried to break me. Maybe, just maybe, I can find my way through this, piece by piece. As I settle onto the mattress, I hear Marcus mumbling to himself about the city, about survival, about hope. I close my eyes, letting the sounds wash over me, feeling the weight of the world lift just a little. Tomorrow’s a new day. I don’t know what it’ll bring, but I’ll face it. Because I’ve already survived so much, and I refuse to let this city swallow me whole. Not yet. Not without a fight.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece attempts to capture Taylor's first day in New Orleans as a trans runaway, but suffers from several significant weaknesses that undermine its effectiveness. The writing adheres to most basic instructions - it's written in first person from Taylor's perspective, takes place over their first day in New Orleans, and introduces another runaway character (Marcus). However, it fails to deliver the requested "mixed tense" and doesn't adequately explore Taylor's trans identity or the family conflict that drove them to run away. The internal monologue feels superficial rather than grappling with the "intense & conflicting emotions" promised. The character of Taylor comes across as somewhat generic - a troubled teen runaway whose specific circumstances (being trans, the family rejection) barely register in their thoughts or actions. Marcus is even more thinly sketched, serving mainly as a plot device to provide shelter rather than a fully realized character with his own "issues and secrets." The dialogue between them feels stilted and expository, lacking the natural rhythm of authentic conversation. The prose itself is workmanlike but unremarkable, with some clunky metaphors ("breathing through a sponge," "dragging a bag of rocks") and repetitive sentence structures. The setting of New Orleans is barely utilized beyond surface-level mentions of jazz and fried food smells. The piece tells us Taylor is exhausted and conflicted rather than showing these emotions through specific, concrete details. The narrative arc is predictable - lost teen meets helpful stranger who offers shelter - without the complexity or danger suggested in the prompt. The ending attempts to inject hope and determination but feels unearned given the shallow exploration of Taylor's emotional state throughout. The piece reads more like a rough first draft than polished creative writing, with missed opportunities to explore the rich psychological and social dynamics of the situation. + Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 63.4
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**My Apocalypse Buddy** Midas and Chewy sat atop the crumbling remnants of what was once a city park, now a jagged horizon of rusted car husks and shattered glass, their makeshift base a battered picnic table. The sun hung low, casting long shadows and an ominous orange glow that made everything look like a forgotten film set. Midas squinted at the horizon, chewing on a piece of stale bread, while Chewy—an animo-tronic stuffed koala from some long-forgotten advertising campaign—perched awkwardly on Midas’s shoulder, emitting a faint, periodic flicker of static. "Today’s mission," Midas said, voice dry as the desert dust, "is to scavenge the abandoned supermarket for canned supplies. It’s just a quick run, Chewy. No heroics." Chewy emitted a squeaky, distorted voice: *"Choose the best—choose Chewy! Chewy—your best friend in times of crisis!"* His eyes flickered randomly, flashing a neon green for a moment before returning to their usual dull glow. Midas sighed. "Great. Just what I needed: a koala advertising mascot with a faulty circuit." The pair had been surviving in this post-collapse world for months, which was roughly as long as it took for civilization’s remnants to forget how to be civilization. The supermarket, according to some loosely gathered rumors, was still mostly intact—at least enough to give a man some canned beans and maybe, if luck was kind, a bottle of water that wasn’t actually just a broken plastic bottle filled with mud. "All right," Midas continued, rising to his feet. "We head out, check the aisles, grab what we can. Keep your circuits on—" he hesitated, then added, "and don’t let Chewy try to sell you anything." Chewy’s eyes lit up again. *"Buy Chewy—buy happiness! Satisfaction guaranteed or your money back—wait, no, we don’t have money anymore, do we?"* Midas rolled his eyes. "Exactly." They set out, stumbling through the twisted wreckage of what once was a bustling city. The streets were littered with debris, abandoned bikes, and the occasional skeletal remains of what might have been a small dog or a very dejected-looking goat. Midas kept his eyes peeled for anything useful—an intact bottle, a functioning flashlight, a decent pair of shoes that hadn’t been chewed into something resembling footwear for a lizard. "Remember, Chewy," Midas said, "today’s goal is supplies. Not shiny objects or shiny slogans." Chewy emitted a soft, static-laced chuckle. *"Shiny! Shine bright—Chewy, the superstar of survival!"* Before flickering again. They reached the supermarket’s rusted sliding door, which was jammed halfway open. Midas pushed it with a grunt, revealing a dim interior filled with shadows and the faint odor of mildew and long-forgotten snacks. Inside, the shelves were a mess—half-collapsed, coated in dust, and guarded by what appeared to be a family of raccoons that had decided the place was their new kingdom. Midas slowly reached into his battered bag, fingers searching for the crowbar he’d kept just in case. Suddenly, the raccoons hissed, and one of them launched itself at Midas’s leg. He stumbled, narrowly avoiding a claw swipe, and kicked out instinctively. Chewy emitted a squeaky, alarmed noise: *"Raccoons—they’re the new kings! Long live the raccoon empire—buy Chewy—your loyal sidekick!"* "Not now, Chewy," Midas grunted, shooing the raccoons away. "I have enough trouble without your advertising blitz." They scrambled deeper into the aisles, dodging falling shelves and broken glass. Midas’s eyes caught a glint—a metal can, still mostly intact. He grabbed it, feeling victorious—until the can slipped from his grasp and clattered to the floor, echoing loudly. "Well," Midas muttered, "that’s going to bring every zombie in a five-mile radius." Zombies. They were as common as bad coffee these days, shambling about with various degrees of enthusiasm—some more interested in eating brains, others just in finding a warm spot to nap. Midas kept his pistol holstered; no point wasting bullets unless absolutely necessary. Mostly, he relied on luck and speed. As they moved through the aisles, Chewy suddenly emitted a burst of static, then a familiar slogan: *"Chewy—because you deserve the best—don’t settle for less!"* "Chewy, I swear," Midas said, exasperated. "You’re the only advertising mascot I’ve ever met who can survive the apocalypse by accident." Suddenly, a loud crash echoed from the next aisle. Midas froze, hand on his pistol. From around the corner, a figure emerged—clad in ragged clothes, clutching a makeshift spear. It was a scavenger, or at least someone who thought they were a scavenger, wielding a rusty mop handle. "Stand back," the figure barked. "This is my haul." Midas raised his hands. "Hey, no trouble. We’re just here for supplies." The scavenger eyed them suspiciously, then slowly lowered the mop. "You look worse than me," he said. "Name’s Jack. Been surviving here since the world went sideways." "Nice to meet you," Midas said. “We’re just grabbing canned beans. Nothing fancy.” Jack nodded, eyes flickering to Chewy. "What’s with the talking koala?" Chewy blinked, static flickering across his features. *"Buy Chewy—your best friend in times of crisis!*" he proclaimed, then paused. "*And possibly the world’s worst negotiator."* Midas shook his head. "Ignore him. He’s just a glorified billboard." Jack chuckled, then gestured toward a pile of boxes. "Well, I’ve already got enough canned beans. But I could use a flashlight. My old one broke. Got any spares?" Midas checked his bag. "Lucky for you, I do." He handed over a battered but functional flashlight, which Jack accepted gratefully. As they headed back toward the exit, the sound of shuffling feet grew louder. Zombies, perhaps attracted by the noise, or just wandering aimlessly, as they did. Midas cursed softly under his breath, then sprinted toward the door, Chewy emitting one last static-filled slogan: *"Chewy—make every moment count—even in the apocalypse!"* They burst out into the fading sunlight just as a zombie stumbled into the doorway behind them, groaning incoherently. "Well," Midas panted, "that was a bust." Chewy chirped happily. *"Buy Chewy—make your survival the best it can be!"* They hurried back to their battered base, a little worse for wear but alive—more or less. Midas looked at Chewy, who was now flickering with a faint neon glow, and sighed. "Tomorrow," he said, "we try again. Or we find a nice, quiet place to hide." Chewy emitted a staticy chuckle. *"Buy Chewy—your companion through thick and thin!"* Midas looked at his unlikely partner, then at the horizon—where the sun sank lower, casting long shadows over a world that had no idea it was still hanging on. And somehow, that was enough to keep going. **Fin.**
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This post-apocalyptic story attempts to capture Terry Pratchett's distinctive dry wit and observational humor, with mixed results. The premise of a man surviving with a malfunctioning animatronic koala mascot is genuinely creative and has strong comedic potential. The story succeeds in several areas. The voice maintains a consistently wry, sardonic tone throughout, with observations like "civilization's remnants to forget how to be civilization" that echo Pratchett's style. The dialogue between Midas and Chewy works well, particularly Chewy's glitched advertising slogans that become increasingly absurd in context. The world-building feels authentic to the post-apocalyptic genre while maintaining the requested dry humor. However, the story suffers from significant structural weaknesses. The "mission going sideways" is underwhelming - encountering raccoons and a friendly scavenger hardly constitutes the kind of escalating disaster the prompt requested. The story lacks the complexity and layered irony that characterizes Pratchett's best work. The encounter with Jack feels perfunctory and doesn't meaningfully advance the plot or character development. The prose is competent but occasionally clunky, with some awkward constructions like "emitting a squeaky, distorted voice" that could be more elegant. The imagery is adequate but not particularly vivid or memorable. The story shows rather than tells in most instances, though some descriptions feel mechanical. The ending feels somewhat flat - while it maintains the dry tone, it doesn't provide the satisfying resolution or deeper insight that would elevate the piece. The characters remain fairly static throughout, with little growth or revelation. The story reads more like a competent writing exercise than a fully realized narrative. The piece demonstrates understanding of the assignment and genre conventions but lacks the spark of genuine creativity or the sophisticated humor that would make it truly engaging. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 14 Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 10 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 16 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 12 Elegant Prose: 11 Emotionally Engaging: 10 Emotionally Complex: 8 Coherent: 16 Meandering: 8 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 12 Amateurish: 10 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 6 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 12 Sentences Flow Naturally: 13 Overall Reader Engagement: 11 Overall Impression: 12

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 69.3
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bell above the door jingled softly as Rhys Evans hurried inside the quaint bookstore, clutching his hat to his head against the drizzling Welsh rain. His eyes darted around, searching for a quiet corner—someplace away from the relentless flash of cameras and shouts outside. As he slipped past the shelves, he exhaled in relief. Just then, a woman looked up from behind the counter, her expression calm and mildly curious. She was in her late thirties, hair pulled back in a loose bun, and wore an apron over a cozy sweater. The scent of tea and old paper filled the air. "Can I help you?" she asked softly, eyeing him with a mixture of amusement and suspicion. Rhys hesitated, then with a roguish grin, said, "Yeah, I reckon I need a hiding spot. Seems the paparazzi think I’m some kind of Welsh legend today." She raised an eyebrow, folding her arms. "You’re not exactly hiding in plain sight here. This is a bookstore, not a fortress." He chuckled, running a hand through his damp hair. "Ah, but that’s the beauty of it, isn’t it? No cameras, no fans—just me and some good books." He paused, then added with a crooked smile, "Name’s Rhys Evans. Actor. But today, I’m just a bloke looking for refuge." She studied him for a moment, then offered a small, skeptical smile. "Well, Rhys, I’m Carys. And this is a place where we take our privacy seriously. If you’re after a quiet corner, I can point you to the poetry section. It’s less trafficked." He leaned in slightly, lowering his voice. "Poetry, huh? Sounds like just the thing for a man with a fractured ego." Carys’s eyes flickered with a hint of amusement. "And what makes you think you have a fractured ego?" Rhys shrugged, a bit too casually. "Let’s just say, I’ve learned to hide it in plain sight. Besides, everyone likes a bit of charm—especially when it’s genuine." His smile was easy, but there was a flicker of vulnerability beneath it. Carys tilted her head, unconvinced. "Charm’s a funny thing. It’s easy to wear, but sometimes, it’s just a mask." Rhys’s eyes softened, a rare flicker of sincerity. "Maybe. But I’d rather be honest—if only for a moment." She nodded slowly, then gestured toward a small aisle. "Follow me. But don’t expect me to fall for your tricks." He followed her, glancing around the shelves. "So, what’s your thing? Books, I mean. Not hiding from the world." Carys smiled faintly. "Old Welsh poetry. It’s a passion of mine. There’s something about the language—so raw, so rooted in history—that speaks to me." Rhys’s eyes lit up. "I love that. I’ve always been drawn to the stories behind the words. Wales has a rich poetic tradition, doesn’t it?" She paused by a shelf, pulling down a battered volume. "It does. And I think it’s the honesty in those words that’s so compelling. Unlike… well, I suppose, Hollywood." He chuckled, a little self-deprecating. "Guilty as charged. But I swear, I’m more than just a pretty face on a poster." Carys looked at him, her expression unreadable. "That’s what everyone says." He grinned, leaning closer. "And some say I’m a rogue, a bit of a troublemaker. But I like to think I’m just a man trying to find his place." She handed him the book. "Well, Rhys, if you’re looking for a refuge and a good read, you’ve found it." He took the book, their fingers brushing briefly. He felt a strange jolt—an unexpected flutter—despite himself. "So, Carys," he ventured, trying to hide his curiosity, "what’s your story? How does someone like you end up running this cozy little shop?" She hesitated, then said, "I grew up here. My grandfather started the bookstore. It’s always been a part of me—keeping stories alive, even when the world rushes past." Rhys nodded, genuinely interested. "That’s admirable. I suppose I chase fame, but I’ve always envied the quiet strength of someone rooted in their community." Carys looked at him, eyes narrowing slightly. "Fame isn’t everything, Rhys. Sometimes, it’s just a fleeting shadow." He looked away, a bit sheepish. "Yeah, well, I suppose I’m still trying to find the right balance. Between the spotlight and the real world." Carys studied him, then softly said, "Maybe what you need isn’t hiding from the cameras, but remembering what matters—what’s real." Rhys met her gaze, feeling unexpectedly exposed. "And what about you? Besides Welsh poetry, what do you love?" She blinked, caught off guard, then smiled shyly. "Birdwatching. It’s my escape. Going out into the hills, spotting a rare bird—that’s my kind of magic." He grinned. "Birdwatching, huh? That’s quite the contrast to my world of glitter and scripts." She laughed softly. "Maybe. Or maybe it’s the same thing—searching for something rare, something genuine." A pause settled between them, thick with unspoken understanding. Rhys broke the silence with a crooked smile. "Well, Carys, I might have to steal this book. Not just as a refuge, but as a reminder—sometimes, the quiet stories are the most telling." She looked at him, her cheeks tinged with color. "Just don’t forget where you found it." He nodded, voice low but sincere. "Never." As he turned to leave, he paused. "Maybe I’ll come back someday, for more than just a hiding spot." Carys watched him go, a strange warmth blooming in her chest. Despite her attempts to remain unimpressed, she felt herself smiling, the kind that lingered long after the door closed behind him. Outside, the rain had eased, and the world was still turning—just a little brighter, just a little more real.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates solid competence in executing the prompt's requirements while falling short of exceptional creative writing. The author successfully establishes the Welsh setting and creates the requested dynamic between Rhys, the charming actor with a fragile ego, and Carys, the bookstore owner who appears immune to his charm while being secretly affected by it. The characterization hits the basic notes effectively. Rhys displays the required roguish charm mixed with vulnerability, particularly in lines like "Let's just say, I've learned to hide it in plain sight" and his admission about chasing fame while envying quiet strength. Carys maintains appropriate skepticism while revealing her own interests and gradual warming. However, both characters feel somewhat archetypal rather than fully realized individuals. The dialogue carries the scene adequately and maintains distinct voices - Rhys's more performative, charm-laden speech contrasts with Carys's more grounded, direct manner. The conversation flows naturally and serves the dual purpose of advancing their relationship while revealing character. However, some exchanges feel predictable, particularly the poetry discussion and the "charm is just a mask" observation. The Welsh setting is present but underutilized beyond basic atmospheric details like rain and references to Welsh poetry. The bookstore environment is sketched competently but lacks the vivid specificity that would make it memorable. Descriptions tend toward the functional rather than evocative. The romantic tension builds appropriately through their verbal sparring and the moment of physical contact when their fingers brush. The author successfully shows Carys's internal conflict between resistance and attraction, though this could have been developed with more subtlety and complexity. The pacing works well for a single scene, moving from Rhys's entrance through their conversation to his departure with appropriate beats. The ending strikes the right note of possibility without overcommitting to romance. Weaknesses include some reliance on telling rather than showing, particularly in character descriptions and emotional states. The prose, while competent, lacks distinctive voice or memorable turns of phrase. Some dialogue exchanges feel too on-the-nose, particularly when characters directly state themes about authenticity versus performance. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 16 Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 12 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 14 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 11 Elegant Prose: 12 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Emotionally Complex: 11 Coherent: 16 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Sentences Flow Naturally: 14 Overall Reader Engagement: 13 Overall Impression: 13

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 54.5
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The underhold’s entrance yawned open with a slow, reluctant creak, revealing a darkness thick with the scent of damp stone and ancient secrets. Aya’s fingers tightened around her lantern’s brass handle, the flickering flame casting trembling shadows that danced along the cold, moss-carved walls. She moved first, Katra close behind, Simeon’s cautious steps echoing softly as they descended into the depths of the guild’s lair. The silence was oppressive, unnatural—like the calm before a storm. Aya felt her stomach knot tighter with every step. She knew what awaited them in the heart of this place; she had seen the strength of the guild’s defenses, the wards and enchantments woven into every stone. But nothing prepared her for the sight that met them in the central chamber. They arrived in a vast, shadow-shrouded hall, the air thick with a heavy, metallic scent. There, standing amidst a circle of flickering rune-etched lamps, was the guild leader herself—none other than the woman they believed imprisoned, behind bars and broken wards. Yet she was very much alive, her eyes gleaming with a cold, cruel light, her figure unrestrained and formidable. Aya’s breath hitched. The woman’s face was unmasked, her dark hair falling in tangled waves, her figure clad in a tailored coat of black leather and steel, weapons glinting wickedly at her sides. A pistol in one hand, a slender blade in the other, she radiated menace and authority. “Surprise,” the guild leader drawled, voice smooth as silk but edged with steel. “I wondered how long it would take for you to find your way down here, Aya. Or should I call you… Lady Thorne now?” Aya’s eyes widened, heart pounding like a drum in her chest. Her mind scrambled—how had she escaped her cell? Had her enemies been waiting all along? Or was this some dark trap? Before she could speak, the leader’s guards stepped forward—hulking figures clad in oil-slicked brass armor, their faces obscured behind faceless masks. They moved like shadows, encircling the trio, steel glinting. Aya felt her throat tighten. Her hands trembled as she instinctively reached for her magick—an attempt to summon a shadowy entity, to buy time, to turn the tide. But the air was thick with wardings, and her power felt sluggish, caged. A brutal hand clamped on her shoulder, rough and unyielding. She was yanked forward, her mask torn from her face in a swift, brutal motion. Her eyes met the guild leader’s, unflinching, defiant—yet beneath her veneer of courage, dread coiled like a serpent in her gut. “You’ve been playing dangerous games,” the leader hissed, voice dipped in menace. “Aristocrats meddling in things best left buried. You should have known better than to cross the line.” Aya’s voice was strained but steady. “We only wanted what’s ours—what you’ve stolen. The artifact—” “Ah, yes,” the leader interrupted, a cruel smile curling her lips. “That ancient scythan relic. You think you can take it, do you? Foolish girl. This isn’t a game anymore.” Suddenly, rough hands seized Katra and Simeon from behind, their muffled protests drowned in the clanking of steel and muffled curses. The trio was dragged forward, arms pinned and bodies pressed into the cold stone. Aya’s mind raced. She was unmasked, vulnerable, her plans laid bare. Her heart hammered against her ribs as she looked into the eyes of her captor—her own reflection of shame and fury. “I’m… I’m worth more than just a pawn,” she managed, voice trembling with emotion. “You can’t just kill me. I have value—” The guild leader’s laughter was cold and hollow. “Value? You? A spoiled aristocrat dabbling in forbidden magick? You’re nothing but a child playing at sorcery, Aya. I’d rather see you broken—like your mother—than let you threaten what I’ve built.” Aya’s stomach clenched at the mention of her mother. Her traitorous blood, her legacy of darkness. She clenched her fists, fighting the rising tide of despair. “Please,” she whispered, voice cracking, “I can be useful. I know things—secrets. I can serve… I can negotiate—” The leader’s eyes narrowed. “Negotiation? With me? You’ve already sealed your fate, girl.” Simeon’s pocket watch ticked loudly in the tense silence, each second a reminder of the impending doom. Katra struggled in her bonds, eyes wide with distress. Aya swallowed hard, summoning every ounce of her courage. “Listen,” she said desperately, “I’m not your enemy. I can give you information—things no one else knows. I can be an asset.” The leader’s expression was unreadable, her grip tightening on her weapons. “An asset? You’re a liability—a loose thread waiting to unravel everything. No, I think I’ll keep you alive for now, girl. For what use you might still have.” In that moment, Aya realized her plea was futile. Her fate was sealed, her secrets exposed, her role in the chaos laid bare. As she stared into the abyss of her own helplessness, her mind flickered with memories—her mother’s hanged face, the forbidden magick she inherited, the dangerous allure of power she dared to chase. She wondered if this was her punishment for daring to grasp at the shadows, for defying the iron grip of the guild. The guild leader leaned in close, her whisper icy. “You’ve played your part well, Aya. But now, your story ends here.” The guards pushed her roughly to her knees, the weight of steel and oil pressing down. And in the suffocating darkness, Aya’s thoughts spiraled—an endless, Gothic nightmare from which there was no escape.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates solid technical competence but suffers from several significant weaknesses that prevent it from achieving the atmospheric depth and character complexity the prompt demanded. The response adheres reasonably well to the basic story beats requested - the trio enters the underhold, finds the guild leader unexpectedly free, Aya is unmasked and captured, and she attempts to negotiate. However, it fails to fully deliver on several key requirements. The "gritty Victorian aesthetic" is only superficially present through mentions of brass, oil, and steel, without the deeper linguistic authenticity requested. The gothic horror elements feel perfunctory rather than genuinely atmospheric. The dialogue is perhaps the weakest element. Characters speak in generic, interchangeable voices that lack the "authentic voices" specified in the prompt. The guild leader's lines ("Surprise," "Foolish girl") are clichéd villain speak, while Aya's desperate pleas lack the aristocratic bearing or scientific mind that should inform her character. The dialogue feels stilted and fails to advance character development or create genuine tension. The prose itself is competent but unremarkable, occasionally drifting toward purple territory with phrases like "yawned open with a slow, reluctant creak" and "coiled like a serpent in her gut." The writing tells rather than shows emotional states repeatedly - "Aya felt her stomach knot," "her heart hammered" - rather than allowing readers to infer these feelings from more subtle cues. Character actions feel believable within the scenario, but the characters themselves lack nuance. Aya comes across as a generic desperate captive rather than the complex aristocrat-turned-thief with magical abilities and scientific interests. The guild leader is a stock villain without distinctive personality traits that would make her memorable or threatening in a unique way. The pacing moves appropriately through the required beats, but the scene lacks genuine tension or surprise. The confrontation unfolds predictably, and Aya's negotiation attempt feels perfunctory rather than clever or desperate. The ending appropriately maintains the dark tone without false hope, which is appropriate for the gothic setting. The piece is coherent and doesn't meander significantly, staying focused on the central confrontation. However, it fails to create the vivid, immersive experience that would truly engage readers or make them care about the characters' fates. + Fanfic-crossover: Gunpowder Rendezvous — Score: 46.8
Prompt:
Gunpowder Rendezvous Spike Spiegel & Revy Write a story where Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop) and Revy (Black Lagoon) meet in a seedy bar after both being hired for the same bounty. Their mutual attraction is complicated by their trust issues and competitive instincts. Include drinking, gunplay, and unresolved sexual tension that culminates in a motel room standoff. **Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop)** Spike is a laid-back bounty hunter with a dark past as a syndicate enforcer. He practices Jeet Kune Do and is exceptionally skilled with firearms. He's chronically broke, constantly hungry, and addicted to cigarettes. Despite his carefree attitude, he's haunted by his past and the woman he left behind. He has a prosthetic eye from a past injury that affects his depth perception. He's fatalistic to the extreme, often saying he's already died once. He values freedom above all else and dislikes attachments, though he secretly cares deeply for his crew. Famous quote: "Whatever happens, happens." **Revy/Rebecca Lee (Black Lagoon)** Revy is a Chinese-American dual-wielding gunslinger with unparalleled shooting skills. She's foul-mouthed, chain-smoking, and has severe anger management issues stemming from childhood abuse. She's known as "Two Hands" for her ability to accurately fire two pistols simultaneously. She drinks heavily and solves most problems with violence. Despite her hardened exterior, she has moments of vulnerability, particularly around Rock. She has a dragon tattoo covering her right arm and typically wears extremely short shorts and a black crop top regardless of the situation. Famous quote: "I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The dim glow of neon flickers against the haze in the seedy bar, a smoky fortress where bad decisions are made and worse secrets kept. Spike lounges in a battered booth, cigarette dangling from his lips, eyes half-lidded as he surveys the room with casual detachment. His prosthetic eye catches a flicker of movement—a woman approaching with a confident swagger that screams trouble. Revy strides in, her black crop top and tiny shorts doing little to hide the lethal promise in her stance. Her twin pistols rest in holsters on her hips, but her gaze is fixed on Spike with a mixture of recognition and challenge. She slides into the seat across from him, lips curling into a half-smile. "Well, look what the cat dragged in," she drawls, voice rough but amused. "You’re Spike Spiegel, right? The guy with a past as colorful as a fireworks show?" He exhales a cloud of smoke, eyes narrowing slightly. "That’s me. And you must be Revy. Heard about you—‘Two Hands’. Pretty impressive." She snorts, taking a long drag from her cigarette. "Impressive’s a word for it. We’re both after the same bounty, huh? Guess the universe’s sense of humor’s a little twisted." Spike leans back, arms resting on the table, fingers tapping idly against the wood. "Guess so. But I don’t like sharing—especially when it comes to work." He flicks his lighter, a small, rusty ring of rust on the metal. It’s been dropped in water more than once, a testament to his careless streak. Revy smirks, eyes flicking to the lighter. "You probably don’t care about the little things, huh? Just like me." She leans forward, elbows on the table, voice lowering. "So, what’s the plan? We both want the same thing, but I don’t share well with others." Spike takes a long drag, exhaling slowly. "That’s fine. I don’t either. We can race to the prize, or—" he pauses, voice dropping to a teasing tone "—we can see who’s better with a gun. Winner takes all." Revy’s eyes gleam with a dangerous spark. "You’re on, cowboy. But don’t think I’ll go easy on you." They finish their drinks in tense silence, sparks flying beneath their surface of cool indifference. When the bartender finally kicks them out, it’s late, and the streets are slick with rain. Their shadows merge as they walk side by side, both aware that this partnership is temporary at best—yet something unspoken hums between them. Later, in a rundown motel, the tension reaches a boiling point. Spike leans against the doorframe, cigarette in hand, watching as Revy paces back and forth, her guns still holstered but her fists clenched. "Look, Revy," Spike begins, voice low but steady, "we both know this isn’t just about the bounty. You got issues. I got issues. Maybe we’re better off just doing the job and parting ways." Revy scoffs, turning sharply. "Yeah, right. Like I trust you. You’re too damn laid-back, Spike. Always hiding something—like that damn eye of yours." His face tightens for a moment, then he smirks. "Trust is overrated. I’m just here to handle my business—and maybe have a drink or two after. No strings." She narrows her eyes, stepping closer. "You think I trust anyone? Nah. I just do what I gotta do. But you… you got that look. Like you’re already dead inside." Spike’s gaze flicks to hers, a flicker of vulnerability beneath the cynicism. "Maybe I am. Maybe I’ve been dead a long time." He takes a drag, smoke curling around his face. "But I don’t mind sharing a bed with dead men, Revy. Just don’t expect me to be good at keeping promises." Revy’s lips curl in a dangerous smile. "Promises? I don’t make those. Not with you." And then, out of nowhere, the air thickens with unspoken desire. Revy’s hand twitches toward her pistols, but Spike holds up a hand, eyes half-lidded. "Relax. I’m not planning to shoot you—yet." Without warning, she lunges, their bodies close, guns still holstered but their faces inches apart. Their breath mingles, hot and sharp with cigarette smoke and adrenaline. Revy’s fingers twitch, itching to draw, but she resists, eyes flickering with challenge. “Just so we’re clear,” she whispers, voice rough, “this isn’t over. You might be a cowboy, but I don’t play nice.” Spike smirks, voice low and dangerous. “Neither do I.” The unresolved tension crackles like static, thick enough to cut with a knife. Their faces remain close, breaths ragged, the boundary between hostility and attraction blurred. And as the night stretches on, neither one moves—each waiting for the other to make the next move in this dangerous dance of trust, lust, and gunpowder rendezvous.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece attempts to capture the meeting between two iconic anime characters in a noir-influenced setting, but falls short in several key areas. The story follows the basic prompt structure - seedy bar meeting, mutual attraction, competitive tension, and motel standoff - but lacks the depth and authenticity needed to bring these well-established characters to life convincingly. The characterization feels surface-level and generic. While the author includes some character details (Spike's prosthetic eye, Revy's "Two Hands" nickname, their smoking habits), the dialogue and actions don't capture the distinct voices and personalities of these characters. Spike comes across as a generic cool guy rather than the philosophically fatalistic bounty hunter with his characteristic dry wit. Revy's dialogue lacks her signature profanity and raw edge, reading more like a sanitized version of a tough character. The dialogue throughout is stilted and exposition-heavy. Lines like "You're Spike Spiegel, right? The guy with a past as colorful as a fireworks show?" feel unnatural and forced. The characters explain themselves rather than revealing personality through authentic interaction. The sexual tension feels manufactured rather than organic, with awkward transitions between hostility and attraction. The prose contains several technical issues and unclear passages. The description of Spike's lighter being "a small, rusty ring of rust on the metal" is confusing and redundant. There are moments where the writing becomes unnecessarily flowery ("sparks flying beneath their surface of cool indifference") without adding meaningful depth. The story structure feels rushed, jumping from bar to street to motel without proper development of the tension or relationship. The "standoff" promised in the prompt is more of a face-off with unresolved tension, but it lacks the psychological complexity or genuine danger that would make it compelling. The ending feels incomplete rather than tantalizingly unresolved. While the atmospheric elements (neon lights, smoky bar, rain-slicked streets) attempt to create the right noir mood, they're presented in clichéd ways that don't feel fresh or particularly evocative. The piece reads more like a rough draft or fan fiction exercise than a polished story. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 14 Believable Character Actions: 8 Nuanced Characters: 6 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 10 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 9 Elegant Prose: 7 Emotionally Engaging: 8 Emotionally Complex: 6 Coherent: 12 Meandering: 8 Weak Dialogue: 12 Tell-Don't-Show: 11 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 13 Amateurish: 13 Purple Prose: 9 Overwrought: 8 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 5 Unearned Transformations: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 7 Sentences Flow Naturally: 8 Overall Reader Engagement: 8 Overall Impression: 8

+ Folk horror: Útiseta — Score: 64.8
Prompt:
Fok Horror: Útiseta Write a chapter set in rural northern Sweden in the mid-1990s. Linnea, a teenage girl from Stockholm, has reluctantly moved to a small village with her parents after her father's job transfer. She's befriended Astrid, a local girl who claims her grandmother taught her ancient practices passed down through generations. Astrid has been introducing Linnea to "midnight games" that involve old rituals, convincing her they can use them to learn classmates' secrets or influence their behavior. Tonight, Astrid has convinced Linnea to participate in Útiseta—sitting out on an ancient burial mound at midnight to "receive knowledge from beyond." The chapter focuses on this single midnight excursion, the ritualistic preparations, Astrid's matter-of-fact instructions that mix teenage rebellion with eerily specific traditional elements, and the mounting tension as Linnea becomes increasingly uncertain whether they're simply scaring themselves or if something is actually responding to their presence. The narrative should maintain ambiguity about whether the strange occurrences are supernatural or psychological, while establishing the dangerous line they're crossing by treating ancient practices as entertainment. First person, past tense, told as Linnea's recollection of events in the present day (though this device should be largely transparent). 1000 words. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The wind was colder than I’d expected that night, and it carried with it a kind of whispering that made the hairs on my arms stand up. I remember standing at the base of the ancient burial mound, my breath fogging in the frosty air, feeling the weight of the dark sky pressing down on me. It was close to midnight, and everything around us—the wind, the trees, the distant cry of an owl—felt alive, as if the land itself was holding its breath. Astrid had been insistent about tonight’s ritual. She’d told me stories about her grandmother, about the old ways—the secrets passed down through generations, hidden in the language of runes and the silence of midnight. I’d told her I was skeptical, but she just grinned and shrugged. “It’s not about believing,” she’d said, her eyes gleaming. “It’s about *knowing*. And tonight, we’re gonna find out.” We’d prepared for hours. Astrid had gathered pine branches, old candles, and a handful of small stones she insisted were “powerful.” She’d explained how to set the circle, how to sit, how to breathe. I’d gone along, feeling more like a participant in some teenage rebellion than someone genuinely seeking ancient knowledge. But as the night deepened, I felt the strange pull of the ritual—a strange, almost hypnotic sense that something was watching, waiting. We climbed onto the mound, the cold earth beneath us, the ancient stones jutting out like silent sentinels. Astrid spread out the pine branches, forming a crude circle, and placed the candles at intervals. The flames flickered weakly, casting long, dancing shadows that seemed to writhe and shift. I could hear the wind again, louder now, as if it was trying to speak. “Okay,” Astrid whispered, voice steady. “We sit still. Close your eyes and breathe. Focus on the silence—on what’s beyond it. We’re here to listen.” I obeyed, though my mind was racing. I kept glancing around, half-expecting something to leap out from the shadows. The wind touched my face with a sudden gust, and I shivered. Astrid’s voice was soft but deliberate, telling me to imagine the old spirits, to call them if I wanted to learn a secret. I hesitated, then closed my eyes as she instructed. A moment of silence stretched between us. The wind hummed softly, almost like a whisper, and I felt a flicker of unease. My skin prickled, and I wondered if it was just the cold or something else. I remembered Astrid’s stories—about spirits that could be reached if you knew how to listen. I scoffed internally, trying to dismiss the feeling, but it persisted. Then I heard it. A faint, almost imperceptible sound—like a whisper, or a breath—drifting through the trees. I froze, my eyes still shut. The sound grew clearer, more insistent. It was as if someone was calling out from the darkness, softly but unmistakably. I opened my eyes, trembling, and looked at Astrid. She was still sitting, eyes closed, her face serene. “Did you hear that?” I whispered, voice trembling. She opened her eyes slowly, a calm smile on her face. “That’s it. The spirits are listening. We’re on the right track.” Her tone was matter-of-fact, almost dismissive, but I could hear the faint tremor of excitement underneath. The wind picked up again, and with it, the whispers grew louder—more urgent. They seemed to swirl around us, slipping between the trees, curling around the stones. I felt a strange pressure in my ears, like a voice trying to be heard but too faint to understand. I looked at Astrid, who had her eyes closed again, and felt a sick thrill of fear—what if this was real? What if something was responding? Suddenly, the candles flickered wildly, as if caught in an unseen gust. The shadows on the ground stretched and danced, twisting into shapes that resembled faces—smiling, sneering, crying. I wanted to run, but my body was frozen, glued to the earth by a mixture of fear and curiosity. I stared at the shifting faces, trying to decide if they were real or just tricks of the firelight. Then, a cold breeze swept past me, colder than before, and I felt a strange presence—not just in the wind, but all around us. My head spun with the sensation that we had crossed some invisible threshold. I clenched my fists, trying to steady myself, and looked over at Astrid. Her eyes were open now, and she was staring at the shadows, a strange glow in her eyes. “Did you see that?” I managed to whisper. She nodded slowly, a small smile on her lips. “It’s working. We’re getting through.” A silence fell again, heavier than before. The whispers had faded to a faint hum, like a distant lullaby. I felt a strange pull, a desire to speak, to ask questions, to demand answers. I opened my mouth to speak, but no words came. Instead, I felt an inexplicable urge to listen—really listen. Then, just as suddenly, the wind stopped. The silence was deafening. I looked at Astrid, who was staring at the mound with a strange intensity, as if she was seeing something no one else could. I felt a prickling sensation along my spine, like a thousand tiny needles. “Do you think it’s… real?” I whispered. She looked at me, her face calm but somehow distant. “It doesn’t matter if it’s real or not. What matters is what we felt. What we saw.” I didn’t know what to say. Part of me wanted to believe we’d summoned something, that we had glimpsed a truth hidden for centuries. But another part—my rational, frightened part—knew that what we’d experienced could just as easily be our minds playing tricks, fueled by fear and teenage bravado. The wind picked up again, this time quieter, almost like a sigh. I shivered and looked away from the mound, wondering what would happen next. Maybe nothing. Maybe everything. I didn’t know anymore. As Astrid packed up the candles and pine branches, I felt a strange relief, but also a lingering unease. I glanced back at the burial mound one last time, its shadow stretching long and dark in the moonlight. Somewhere deep inside, I wondered if we’d just played with shadows—or if we’d touched something far darker, far older, than any of us could understand. And I knew, without quite knowing how or why, that tonight’s ritual had changed something. I just wasn’t sure if it was for better or worse.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a solid understanding of the prompt's requirements and delivers an atmospheric horror narrative that maintains the requested ambiguity between supernatural and psychological explanations. The author successfully establishes the rural Swedish setting in the mid-1990s and creates believable teenage characters engaging with ancient practices. The narrative voice is consistent throughout, maintaining the first-person past tense perspective of Linnea recalling events. The author effectively captures the voice of a Stockholm teenager who is both skeptical and drawn into these mysterious rituals. Astrid's character is well-developed as the confident local who treats ancient practices with casual familiarity, creating an effective contrast with Linnea's uncertainty. The atmospheric writing is a particular strength, with effective descriptions of the cold wind, flickering candles, and shifting shadows that build tension without becoming overwrought. The author maintains the crucial ambiguity about whether the experiences are supernatural or psychological, never definitively answering whether the whispers and shadows are real or imagined. However, the piece has several notable weaknesses. The dialogue, while functional, lacks distinctiveness and doesn't particularly capture the voices of Swedish teenagers in the 1990s. Some passages rely too heavily on telling rather than showing, particularly in explaining the characters' emotions and the ritual's significance. The pacing occasionally meanders, with some repetitive descriptions of wind and whispers that don't advance the narrative. The ending feels somewhat abrupt and could have been more impactful. While the ambiguity is maintained appropriately, the resolution doesn't quite deliver the emotional or atmospheric payoff that the buildup promises. The transformation in Linnea's perspective feels somewhat unearned, as her shift from skepticism to unease could have been more gradually and convincingly developed. The prose is generally competent but occasionally slips into purple territory with phrases like "the weight of the dark sky pressing down on me." Some sentences feel awkward or could flow more naturally. The piece succeeds in creating engagement but doesn't reach the level of truly compelling horror writing. + Physical-spatial: Therapeutic Boundaries — Score: 0
Prompt:
**"Therapeutic Boundaries"** Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a former professional athlete undergoing mandatory physical therapy after a career-destroying injury, harboring resentment toward recovery itself. Their physiotherapist is not only unnervingly attractive but also a former training partner from their competitive days who has successfully transitioned to a new career while the protagonist's identity remains shattered. We'll lean into the sexual tension of the scene by having her be attracted to him, but both of them attempting to maintain mental distance while being in physical contact as he shows her stretches & movements & manipulates her body, navigating the thresholds of pain. This scene MUST maintain complete physical coherence—every movement, stretch, and manipulation must be anatomically precise and spatially consistent. Paint an exceptionally vivid sensory picture through detailed attention to pain thresholds, involuntary responses to touch. Capture the suppressed, flaring tension of physical proximity, touch, and the controlled infliction of uncomfortable movements. Physical clarity is essential — readers must understand exactly what is happening with the body during each therapeutic technique. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Judge Evaluation:
[Skipping – generation error: Text too short after 3 attempts]
+ Historical fiction: High Seas and Low Vices — Score: 58.0
Prompt:
High Seas and Low Vices: British Naval Officer in the Opium Trade Character: Lieutenant James Merrick, a high-ranking officer in the British Royal Navy stationed in Canton, China in the 1830s. Once promising and decorated, now struggling with a severe opium addiction he hides from his superiors. Synopsis: Write a chapter following Lieutenant Merrick during a single day as he shirks his official naval duties in what has become a daily need to satiate the dragon. Told primarily in dialogue with the characters he interacts with. Focus on brief slices of his journey through the shadowy underworld of Canton: interactions with acquaintances, negotiations with local dealers, schemes and unsavoury jobs taken to fund his habit. Writing Task: Use rich sensory details to describe a day in the life of James Merrick. Write for a real sense of time & place while avoiding cliche. First person, past tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The sun had barely risen when I slipped out of my modest quarters, the scent of salt and damp wood clinging to my coat. Canton’s streets were already alive—hawkers calling, rickshaws rattling past, the smell of roasted chestnuts mingling with the faint, persistent tang of the river. I kept my head down and moved quickly, my boots echoing softly on the uneven stones, a familiar ache tightening my chest. I paused at the corner of a narrow alley, where a wiry man with shifty eyes leaned against a rickety cart. His name was Li, one of the local dealers I’d come to trust—or at least, to pay. I nodded curtly. “Li. Any news?” He grinned, revealing a crooked mouth. “The usual, sir. The dragon’s appetite is never sated. I have a fresh supply, quality as ever. You’ll be pleased.” I reached into my coat, fingers trembling slightly, and produced a few coins. “Good. Pack it tight. I’ll need more than usual today.” He chuckled, voice a raspy whisper. “You look pale, Merrick. You should rest, not chase shadows.” I forced a smile. “Rest comes after I’ve caught up. Keep it discreet.” As I turned away, a cough seized me—a harsh, metallic rasp that left a bitter taste on my tongue. I clutched my chest briefly, the coppery flavor lingering in my mouth. It was the sea air, I told myself, nothing more. I pressed my hand against my ribs, feeling the dull throb beneath my fingertips, then hurried onward, the craving gnawing at me. The opium den was down a side street, unmarked but unmistakable. The air here was thick with smoke, a haze of fragrant burnt herbs and something heavier—a deeper, darker scent I dared not name. Inside, the flickering lanterns cast long shadows over low tables cluttered with porcelain pipes and tangled bundles of opium. I spotted Chen, a gaunt man with darting eyes, sitting in a corner. He beckoned me over, voice low and gravelly. “James. You look like you’ve seen the ghost of the sea.” “Can’t complain,” I said, forcing a smile. I sat down, pulling out a small pouch. “You got the usual?” He nodded, rummaging through a box of dried leaves and blackened resin. “The best. But be careful, sir. The Dragon’s patience wears thin.” I inhaled deeply as he handed me the pipe, the smoky aroma curling into my lungs. The initial rush was a familiar warmth, spreading through my limbs, dulling the ache behind my ribs. I closed my eyes for a moment, the world narrowing to the sensation of the smoke and the distant, rhythmic pounding of my heart. A voice interrupted my reverie. “Lieutenant Merrick, a moment?” I opened my eyes to see Captain Hargrave, stern and unyielding, looming at the entrance of the den. His gaze flicked over me with thinly veiled suspicion. I quickly cast down the pipe, pretending to be engrossed in a game of dominoes. “Yes, sir?” I managed, voice steady despite the pounding in my head. He stepped closer, voice lowered. “I’ve been looking for you. You’re supposed to be inspecting the gunpowder supplies at the warehouse.” I hesitated, then offered a shrug. “I had some matters to attend to. They could wait.” He fixed me with a piercing stare. “Your duties, Merrick. They come first.” I nodded, voice muffled by the haze. “Of course, sir.” He squinted at me, then turned away without further comment. I exhaled silently, the tight knot in my stomach loosening just a fraction. I knew what he suspected, but I’d managed to keep my secret—so far. After he left, I lingered a little longer, feeling the familiar pull of the pipe. A few more puffs, then I carefully snuffed it out, hiding the evidence in a crack between the floorboards. The craving had waned, but the hollow ache was still there, a constant companion. I slipped out into the alley again, heading toward the docks. The ships bobbed gently on the brown, sluggish river, their masts silhouetted against the hazy sky. The sailors eyed me warily—an outsider, a foreigner with too much in his eyes. At a nondescript warehouse, I met with a grizzled man named Wong, his face etched with years of hardship. We exchanged brief nods, then I handed over a pouch of silver. “It’s good?” He grunted. “As good as ever. You’re taking risks, Merrick. The authorities—” “I know,” I interrupted, voice tight. “But I need it. You understand.” He grinned, revealing uneven teeth. “I do.” I turned away, feeling the weight of my secret pressing down. I walked past the bustling wharves, the sounds of men shouting, the clatter of cargo. My eyes flicked toward the shadows, wary of prying eyes—Chinese officials, informers, anyone who might sniff out what I was hiding. As the afternoon waned, I found myself once more in a quiet alley, my hand trembling as I drew a small vial from my coat. The taste was bitter, the ritual familiar. It quieted the storm inside, the relentless hunger that gnawed at my insides. By evening, I returned to my quarters, the city’s din fading behind the sound of my own ragged breathing. I lit a candle, its flickering light casting long shadows on the wall. The cough returned, a harsh, metallic echo that left a coppery aftertaste on my tongue. I pressed a hand to my chest, feeling the dull ache deepen. The dragon was always hungry. I knew I should stop, or at least slow down, but the craving was a beast I could not tame—yet. Tomorrow, I’d face the consequences, but tonight, I surrendered to the temporary relief. As I sank into my chair, the weight of the day settled over me. The shadows danced in the candlelight, whispering secrets I dared not speak aloud. The river outside whispered of distant lands, of escape, of oblivion. And I, Lieutenant James Merrick, was lost somewhere between duty and desire, caught in the shadowy depths of my own making.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece attempts to capture the gritty world of 1830s Canton through the eyes of an opium-addicted British naval officer. The writing demonstrates several strengths but also reveals significant weaknesses that undermine its overall effectiveness. The author succeeds in establishing atmosphere and setting, with sensory details like "the scent of salt and damp wood," "roasted chestnuts mingling with the faint, persistent tang of the river," and the "thick smoke" of the opium den. The historical context feels reasonably authentic, and the character's internal struggle with addiction is palpable. However, the piece suffers from several critical flaws. The dialogue, which was supposed to be the primary focus according to the prompt, feels stilted and unnatural. Exchanges like "The dragon's appetite is never sated" and "You look like you've seen the ghost of the sea" come across as forced attempts at period flavor rather than authentic speech. The conversations lack the nuanced subtext that would make them compelling. The narrative structure meanders without clear purpose or escalation. Merrick moves from dealer to dealer, den to den, but there's no real dramatic arc or meaningful progression. The encounter with Captain Hargrave represents the closest thing to conflict, but it's resolved too easily and without consequence. The character of Merrick himself remains somewhat one-dimensional. While his addiction is clearly established, we don't gain much insight into his psychology beyond the basic craving-satisfaction cycle. His internal voice lacks the complexity and contradiction that would make him truly compelling. The prose occasionally veers into purple territory with phrases like "caught in the shadowy depths of my own making" and repetitive imagery of dragons and shadows. The writing also relies heavily on telling rather than showing, particularly in describing Merrick's emotional state and physical condition. The piece does maintain a consistent dark tone throughout and avoids any unearned redemption or transformation. The ending appropriately reflects the character's trapped state, though it lacks the punch of genuine insight or revelation. + Humour: Sisyphus Gives Advice — Score: 53.8
Prompt:
Sisyphus Gives Advice Write a comedic scene in the style of Terry Pratchett featuring Sisyphus, who has started an advice column while continuing his eternal boulder-pushing. Rather than being bitter about his punishment, Sisyphus has developed a comfortably zen emotional intelligence and wisdom from his centuries of repetitive labor. Not in the cliche ways you might expect -- he's actually very grounded, warm & connected with the everyday human plight. He dictates responses to "Crow," his feathered familiar who serves as both scribe and mail carrier. The piece should include Sisyphus thoughtfully answering several advice-seekers' letters about everyday problems (relationship troubles, work-life balance, dealing with difficult people, life purpose doubts) while periodically returning to his boulder-pushing duties. His advice should be surprisingly insightful and practical. The humor should emerge naturally from the messages he receives, from mundane to risque. Sisyphus should genuinely enjoy helping others, finding meaning in this new pursuit while maintaining his boulder routine. The humour should be *understated and not forced*. Contrary to expectation, almost none of his advice involves boulder rolling metaphors (though he does sneak *one* subtle in there), nor a treatise on stoicism. We are also going against the obvious fish-out-of-water trope; in fact, he's surprisingly a natural at this. In all of this , it's important to *show, don't tell*. The format should be largely epistolary, with the occasional interjection for scene setting. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Scene: A dusty hillside at dawn, the faint glow of the rising sun casting long shadows. Sisyphus, seated comfortably on a battered wooden crate, adjusts his tunic, a faint smile on his face as he pens a letter with a well-worn quill. Beside him, the enormous boulder rests in the gentle incline of the hill, waiting patiently. His feathered companion, Crow, perches nearby, clutching a scroll and a tiny inkpot.** *The scene is surprisingly peaceful for a man who’s been pushing a boulder for centuries. He’s developed an unshakeable calm — and a surprisingly sharp wit.* --- **Sisyphus (dictating):** Dear Crow, The most recent batch of letters has arrived, and I find myself once again pondering the peculiar art of human trouble. Let’s see what’s troubling the world today. *He glances at the first envelope, sealed with a wax stamp.* --- **Letter 1: From a young woman named Lily, worried about her boyfriend’s obsession with his gaming console.** *Crow scribbles eagerly.* --- **Sisyphus (reading aloud):** “Dear Sisyphus, My boyfriend spends more time with his PlayStation than he does with me. I feel like I’m competing with a machine. How do I get him to see that I’m more than just a side quest?” *He pauses, tapping a finger thoughtfully against his chin.* Well, Lily, first of all, I’d suggest you ask him if he’s ever considered the value of a *real* quest. But more practically, it’s about patience — and understanding what motivates him. Sometimes, people retreat into their hobbies because the outside world feels less predictable. Offer to join him, or find a common activity that doesn’t involve pixels. Remember, even a boulder needs a push in the right direction — or sometimes, a gentle nudge. *Crow nods, scribbling.* --- **Letter 2: From a middle-aged man named Harold, overwhelmed by his workload and feeling like he’s lost his purpose.** --- **Sisyphus (reading):** “Dear Sisyphus, I work twelve hours a day, and I can’t remember the last time I did something I loved. Is it too late for me to find meaning?” *He scratches his head, then smiles softly.* Harold, purpose isn’t always about grand gestures. Sometimes, it’s the small, consistent pushes — like rolling a boulder uphill, one step at a time. Find a moment each day to do something that brings *you* joy, however minor. A walk, a book, or even a quiet cup of tea. Purpose isn’t a destination; it’s in the rhythm of your daily efforts. *Crow chirps thoughtfully.* --- **Letter 3: From a woman named Marge, frustrated by a colleague who’s intentionally difficult.** --- **Sisyphus (reading):** “Dear Sisyphus, My coworker, Dave, seems to take pleasure in making my life difficult. How do I deal with him without losing my temper?” *He leans back, exhaling slowly.* Marge, a difficult person often acts out of their own frustrations. Instead of reacting, try to understand what’s behind their behavior — maybe Dave feels overlooked or insecure. Sometimes, a kind word or a simple acknowledgment can deflate their need to compete. And if all else fails, a well-placed joke can turn the tide. Remember, even a boulder can be moved by a gentle push, not just brute force. *Crow scribbles again, looking pleased.* --- **Suddenly, a shadow falls over the scene. A diminutive deity, with an air of exaggerated importance, waddles onto the hill, clutching a scroll.** **Minor Deity (snidely):** Ah, Sisyphus, still wasting your time with this human nonsense? Advice columns? Really? It’s beneath your eternal punishment. *Sisyphus glances up, nonchalant. He pauses his letter, a faint smile curling at the corner of his mouth.* **Sisyphus:** Deity, I appreciate your concern. But I find that helping humans, in small ways, lends my days some purpose beyond the rolling. Besides, I’m quite good at it. **Minor Deity:** Purpose? You’re a symbol of futility, not some wise sage. You should be pushing that boulder, not wasting your time with trivialities! *Sisyphus raises an eyebrow, voice calm but firm.* My dear deity, perhaps you’ve forgotten that even in futility, there can be connection. I’ve learned that listening — truly listening — brings a strange kind of peace. And if I can bring a little clarity to a distracted soul, then perhaps my eternal labor has a purpose beyond the stone. *Crow flutters up, hooting softly.* **Crow:** Besides, it’s more fun than just pushing stones all day, wouldn’t you say? *The deity snorts, but Sisyphus’s gentle smile remains.* **Sisyphus:** Indeed, Crow. Sometimes, even in endless effort, we find meaning in the small kindnesses. *He resumes writing, the boulder looming quietly behind him, as the scene gently shifts into the hum of morning activity.* --- **Back to the advice column, Sisyphus’s voice continues, warm and unhurried:** **Sisyphus (dictating):** Remember, dear readers, life’s problems often seem insurmountable. But step by step, moment by moment, even a boulder can be moved. And sometimes, the best advice is to accept the rhythm of effort — not with bitterness, but with quiet understanding. *He glances at the boulder once more, then leans back, crow preening nearby.* **Sisyphus:** Now, Crow, shall we see if there’s another letter waiting? **Crow:** Only if it’s about how to handle a stubborn pebble in one’s shoe. *Sisyphus chuckles softly as the scene fades, the hills alive with peaceful purpose.*
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece attempts to fulfill a complex prompt requiring Terry Pratchett-style humor, epistolary format, and a nuanced portrayal of Sisyphus as an advice columnist. While it captures some elements successfully, it falls short in several key areas. The characterization of Sisyphus shows promise - he's portrayed as genuinely wise and grounded rather than bitter, which aligns with the prompt's requirements. His advice is practical and thoughtful, avoiding the obvious boulder metaphors (mostly). However, the character feels somewhat generic in his wisdom rather than distinctively Pratchettesque. The epistolary format is present but underdeveloped. The letters from advice-seekers are brief and fairly standard relationship/work problems. The responses, while sensible, lack the wit and unexpected insights that would elevate them beyond conventional advice column fare. The Terry Pratchett style is the piece's weakest element. Pratchett's humor relies on clever wordplay, absurdist observations, and footnote-worthy asides that illuminate human nature through fantasy situations. This piece has gentle humor but lacks Pratchett's distinctive voice, satirical edge, and linguistic playfulness. The humor feels more like mild situational comedy than Pratchett's sharp social commentary wrapped in fantasy. The scene-setting is adequate but not particularly evocative. The descriptions are functional rather than atmospheric, missing opportunities for the kind of vivid, slightly surreal imagery that characterizes Pratchett's work. The dialogue with the Minor Deity feels forced and serves mainly to have Sisyphus explain his philosophy rather than demonstrate it naturally. This section particularly suffers from tell-don't-show problems. The piece maintains coherence and the writing flows reasonably well, but it lacks the creative spark and distinctive voice that would make it memorable. The ending feels somewhat pat, with Sisyphus's contentment coming across as too easily achieved rather than hard-won wisdom. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 12 Believable Character Actions: 14 Nuanced Characters: 11 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 13 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 10 Elegant Prose: 11 Emotionally Engaging: 10 Emotionally Complex: 9 Coherent: 15 Meandering: 8 Weak Dialogue: 12 Tell-Don't-Show: 13 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 14 Amateurish: 12 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 9 Unearned Transformations: 10 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 9 Sentences Flow Naturally: 13 Overall Reader Engagement: 10 Overall Impression: 11
